summary: Jeff Atwood explores the history and significance of the Hello World program, tracing its origins from the 1978 C Programming Language book back to a 1974 Bell Labs memorandum by Kernighan. He notes that while Hello World appears simple, it serves as a crucial sanity test for verifying that a complete programming toolchain is correctly installed. The post highlights the surprising complexity that can exist in GUI contexts and embedded systems, and points to the extensive catalog of Hello World implementations across hundreds of languages, including joke languages. Atwood closes by noting the existence of Hello World performance benchmarks, which he finds amusingly useless but fitting.
key_points: Hello World programs serve as essential sanity tests for validating that compilers, development environments, and runtimes are correctly configured
The tradition originated from Kernighan's 1974 Bell Labs tutorial and was popularized by the 1978 C Programming Language book
Despite their simplicity, Hello World programs can be surprisingly complex in GUI contexts or embedded systems
Setting up a complete programming toolchain from scratch to run even trivial programs can involve substantial work
The existence of Hello World performance benchmarks is acknowledged as completely useless but fitting for the spirit of the exercise
money_quotes: Configuring a complete programming toolchain from scratch to the point where even trivial programs can be compiled and run may involve substantial amounts of work.
Of course, it's a completely useless performance test. But that's very much in the spirit of Hello World.
The archetypal Hello World program has always had a calming effect on developers.
A 'hello world' program can be a useful sanity test to make sure that a language's compiler, development environment, and run-time environment are correctly installed.
Some, however, are surprisingly complex, especially in some GUI contexts; but most are very simple.
key_insight: The humble Hello World program, despite its apparent triviality, serves as a meaningful validation that an entire programming toolchain is working correctly—making it far more valuable than its simplicity suggests.
